title: Market Failure, Externalities & Information
simple_html:
Free markets usually allocate resources well — but not always. Market failure is any situation where a competitive market, left alone, produces an outcome that is inefficient (too much or too little of something, or nothing at all). Understanding failure tells managers and policymakers when and how to intervene.

The main types

  • Externalities — costs or benefits that spill over onto third parties. Pollution is a negative externality (the factory ignores the harm to neighbors, so it produces too much). Vaccination is a positive externality (people ignore the benefit to others, so too few get vaccinated).
  • Public goods — things that are non-excludable (you can't stop people using them) and non-rival (one person's use doesn't reduce another's), like national defense or street lighting. Because everyone can free-ride, markets under-provide them.
  • Asymmetric information — one side of a deal knows more than the other. A used-car seller knows the car's faults; the buyer doesn't.

Fixing it

Common remedies: taxes on harmful activities (a Pigouvian tax), subsidies for beneficial ones, assigning clear property rights so parties can bargain (the Coase idea), government provision of public goods, and rules that improve information disclosure.

Key takeaways

  • Markets fail with externalities, public goods, and information gaps.
  • Negative externalities cause over-production; positive ones cause under-production.
  • Free-riding makes markets under-supply public goods.
  • Information problems create adverse selection and moral hazard.
intermediate_html:
Market failure occurs when a competitive market, despite functioning, allocates resources inefficiently — the equilibrium quantity diverges from the socially optimal one. The first welfare theorem says competitive markets are efficient only under strong assumptions; market failure is what happens when those assumptions break. The main sources are externalities, public goods, and asymmetric information.

Externalities

An externality is a cost or benefit imposed on a third party not reflected in the market price, so private and social costs diverge. With a negative externality (e.g., pollution), marginal social cost (MSC) > marginal private cost (MPC); the market produces where price equals MPC, over-producing relative to the social optimum where price equals MSC. The gap creates a deadweight loss. With a positive externality (e.g., education, vaccination), marginal social benefit exceeds private benefit and the market under-produces.

Remedies. A Pigouvian tax equal to the marginal external damage at the optimum internalizes the cost, shifting the supply curve up so private actors face true social cost; a subsidy does the reverse for positive externalities. Alternatively, the Coase Theorem (Coase, 1960) argues that if property rights are well-defined and bargaining is costless, private parties will negotiate to the efficient outcome regardless of who holds the right — so the policy priority may be assigning and enforcing rights (and reducing transaction costs) rather than taxing. Tradable permit systems (cap-and-trade) operationalize this for pollution.

Worked example: Pigouvian tax

Suppose private supply is MPC = 10 + Q, demand is P = 100 − Q, and each unit imposes external damage of 20, so MSC = 30 + Q. Market equilibrium: 100 − Q = 10 + Q → Q = 45. Social optimum: 100 − Q = 30 + Q → Q = 35. The market over-produces by 10 units. A tax of 20 per unit raises private cost to MSC and moves output to the efficient 35.

Public goods

Public goods are non-excludable and non-rival (Samuelson, 1954). Non-excludability invites free-riding — consuming without paying — so private firms cannot capture enough revenue and under-provide. The efficient quantity satisfies the Samuelson condition: the sum of individuals' marginal benefits equals marginal cost (because all consume the same unit). Typical solutions: government provision funded by taxes, or mechanisms that induce truthful demand revelation.

Asymmetric information

ProblemWhen it arisesExampleFix
Adverse selectionHidden information before the dealLemons market; sick people buy more insuranceSignaling, screening, warranties
Moral hazardHidden action after the dealInsured driver takes more riskDeductibles, monitoring, incentives

Akerlof's "Market for Lemons" (Akerlof, 1970) shows adverse selection can unravel a market entirely: if buyers cannot tell good cars from bad, they offer only average prices, good sellers exit, quality falls, and the market collapses. Remedies include signaling by the informed party (Spence, 1973 — e.g., education or warranties) and screening by the uninformed party (Stiglitz — e.g., insurance menus that sort risk types).

How to apply it

  • Diagnose the source — externality, public good, or information gap — before prescribing a fix.
  • Match the tool: Pigouvian tax/subsidy or tradable permits for externalities; public provision for public goods; disclosure, signaling, and incentive contracts for information problems.
  • Weigh transaction costs: Coasean bargaining works only when rights are clear and bargaining is cheap.
full_html:
Market failure is the situation in which a competitive market, operating freely, fails to allocate resources Pareto-efficiently. It is the conceptual boundary of the laissez-faire case: the First Fundamental Theorem of Welfare Economics guarantees that competitive equilibrium is efficient only when markets are complete, competitive, and informationally symmetric. Market failure catalogues the breakdowns of those conditions and grounds the economic case for policy intervention. The principal categories are externalities, public goods, and asymmetric information (market power, treated in Lesson 6, is a fourth source).

Externalities

An externality exists when one agent's action confers a cost or benefit on others that is not transmitted through prices, driving a wedge between private and social valuations. For a negative externality (pollution, congestion), marginal social cost (MSC) = marginal private cost (MPC) + marginal external cost (MEC). The unregulated market equilibrates where demand meets MPC, producing Q_market > Q_social (where demand meets MSC), generating a deadweight loss equal to the triangle of units whose social cost exceeds social benefit. Positive externalities (vaccination, R&D spillovers, education) reverse the logic: marginal social benefit exceeds marginal private benefit, and the market under-produces.

The Pigouvian solution. Pigou (1920) proposed a corrective tax equal to the marginal external cost evaluated at the social optimum, internalizing the externality so private agents face true social cost; symmetrically, a Pigouvian subsidy corrects under-provision. The practical difficulty is measuring the external cost and setting the rate correctly.

The Coasean critique. Coase (1960), in "The Problem of Social Cost," argued that externalities are reciprocal and that, if property rights are well-defined and transaction costs are zero, private bargaining will reach the efficient allocation regardless of the initial assignment of rights — the celebrated Coase Theorem. The distribution of gains depends on who holds the right, but efficiency does not. The deep implication is that market failure often reflects missing or ill-defined property rights and high transaction costs rather than externalities per se. Where transaction costs are high (many parties, measurement problems), bargaining fails and Pigouvian instruments or tradable permits (cap-and-trade, which create property rights in pollution and let the market price them) become attractive — combining Coasean rights with market allocation (Dales, 1968; Montgomery, 1972).

Worked example: correcting a negative externality

Let demand be P = 120 − Q, private marginal cost MPC = 20 + Q, and a constant marginal external cost MEC = 30, so MSC = 50 + Q. The free-market equilibrium solves 120 − Q = 20 + Q → Q_m = 50, P = 70. The social optimum solves 120 − Q = 50 + Q → Q* = 35, P = 85. The market over-produces by 15 units; the deadweight loss is ½ × MEC × (Q_m − Q*) = ½ × 30 × 15 = 225. A Pigouvian tax of 30 per unit raises private cost to MSC and restores the efficient output of 35. This is the template for carbon taxes and congestion charges.

Public goods

Samuelson (1954) formalized public goods as those that are non-rival (one person's consumption does not diminish another's) and non-excludable (no one can be cost-effectively excluded). Examples: national defense, basic research, lighthouses, clean air. Non-excludability creates the free-rider problem: each individual benefits whether or not they pay, so voluntary contributions fall short and private markets under-provide or fail to provide at all. Efficiency requires the Samuelson condition: the vertical sum of individuals' marginal rates of substitution (sum of marginal willingness to pay) equals the marginal cost of provision — Σ MB_i = MC — in contrast to private goods, where each consumer's MB equals price. Solutions include tax-financed government provision, assurance contracts, and demand-revealing mechanisms (e.g., the Clarke-Groves mechanism), though all struggle with preference revelation. A 2×2 of rivalry and excludability also locates common-pool resources (rival but non-excludable — fisheries, grazing land), which suffer the "tragedy of the commons" (Hardin, 1968); Ostrom (1990) showed communities can self-govern these through institutions, a Nobel-recognized qualification to Hardin's pessimism.

Asymmetric information

When one party to a transaction holds private information, markets can be inefficient or collapse. Two distinct problems arise depending on timing:

Adverse selection (hidden information, pre-contract). Akerlof's "The Market for Lemons" (Akerlof, 1970) showed that when sellers know quality but buyers do not, buyers pay only the average value; high-quality sellers withdraw, average quality falls, prices drop further, and the market can unravel entirely. The same logic explains why the sick disproportionately buy health insurance, raising premiums and driving out the healthy. Remedies operate through information: signaling, where the informed party credibly conveys quality at a cost that only high types will bear (Spence, 1973 — education as a labor-market signal; warranties; brand investment), and screening, where the uninformed party designs a menu that induces self-selection (Rothschild & Stiglitz, 1976 — insurance contracts that separate risk types via deductible/premium trade-offs).

Moral hazard (hidden action, post-contract). Once insured or contracted, an agent may take unobservable actions that raise risk or lower effort because they do not bear the full consequences — an insured driver drives less carefully; a salaried manager shirks. The principal-agent framework (Holmström, 1979; Jensen & Meckling, 1976) addresses this by aligning incentives: deductibles and co-payments, performance-based pay, monitoring, and bonding. The 2001 Nobel Prize to Akerlof, Spence, and Stiglitz recognized this entire information-economics program.

Adverse selectionMoral hazard
Information problemHidden type (quality, risk)Hidden action (effort, care)
TimingBefore contractingAfter contracting
Classic exampleUsed-car lemons; insurance poolInsured risk-taking; managerial shirking
RemediesSignaling, screening, warranties, mandatesDeductibles, monitoring, incentive pay, bonding

Nuances and debates

  • Government failure. Intervention is not costless: regulators face their own information gaps, capture by interest groups (Stigler, 1971), and incentive problems. The relevant comparison is imperfect markets versus imperfect government, not against a frictionless ideal.
  • Coase vs. Pigou. The choice between bargaining (rights-based) and taxation (price-based) hinges on transaction costs, the number of parties, and measurability — a central debate in environmental and law-and-economics.
  • Behavioral market failures. Modern work adds systematic cognitive biases as a source of failure, motivating "nudge" and choice-architecture interventions (Thaler & Sunstein, 2008), which connects to Behavioral Economics.

Common mistakes

  • Confusing inefficiency with mere distributional outcomes. Market failure is about efficiency loss (deadweight loss), not unfairness per se.
  • Setting the Pigouvian tax to the wrong marginal cost. It must equal MEC at the optimum, not at the unregulated quantity.
  • Conflating adverse selection and moral hazard. They differ by timing (before vs. after the contract) and call for different remedies.
  • Assuming intervention always improves welfare. Government failure can exceed the market failure being corrected.
  • Treating all under-provided goods as public goods. Excludability or rivalry alone disqualifies a good; check both properties.

Market failure is the capstone that defines the proper scope of markets and the rationale for policy. It links back to Market Structures (Lesson 6, where monopoly is itself a failure) and to Game Theory (Lesson 8, since free-riding on public goods is a Prisoner's Dilemma and signaling is a Bayesian game), unifying the course's micro foundations into a framework for diagnosing when markets need help and what kind.
